comments.content: I'm about 80% of my way through reading _The Picatrix_ for the first time.  What a fascinating text!  Have you worked making talismans using these methods?  Most, but not all of course, of the elections and talismans described seem to be similar in their effect to many Hoodoo workings.  In your experience, do the demanding elections have a corresponding increase of power in the talismans created?  It seems like so, so, so much hassle and expense, which I imagine could be worth it, but it seems often there could be simpler ways of doing the same thing.  Of course, I'm curious your thoughts of the relative virtues of these two major types of magic that you've studied in depth.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Yes, I used the Picatrix methods extensively during and after the time I spent working with Chris Warnock on the translation, and I found that getting the elections just right really does result in a stronger talisman. Of course there are simpler ways of doing any given working; it depends on how much magical force you need.

The thing to keep in mind is that the Picatrix was written in an era and a culture where magic was everywhere, and the sort of natural magic you find in Hoodoo (among many other traditions) was all but universally practiced. If you wanted to accomplish things in such a setting, you had to have magic that was much more potent -- and that's what the Picatrix provided. It was a handbook for professionals, remember, and taught them how to leverage their knowledge of astrology to wield really potent magical powers.

comments.title: Polytheistic ceremonial magic with Wicca
comments.content:
Hello JMG.

So, I know you’re not a fan of amateurs combining magical systems. However! Considering that the polytheistic rituals you’ve been posting recently are not only new, but also flexible in regards to pantheon choice, I was wondering what is your opinion of practicing the LPR for polytheists alongside Wicca?

Secondly, on a more adventurous note, what is the worst that could happen by practicing this same polytheistic LRP alongside Gates of the Elements from your Druid Magic Handbook?

Many thanks.

replies.title: Re: Polytheistic ceremonial magic with Wicca
replies.content:
1) I don't know enough about Wicca to be able to give a useful answer. It's never appealed to me, and so I haven't studied it enough to know what kind of energies it works with and how well those would interact with the rite I presented.

2) Do you also think that pissing in a pot of coffee before you drink some will improve the taste? One of the dreariest habits of the rank beginner in magic these days is the insistence on not following instructions -- I think it's an ego thing, driven by the discomfort that comes from realizing that someone else really does know more about the subject than you.

That is to say, why don't you put in the really very modest investment of time and effort you'd need to learn the Sphere of Protection instead, so you can actually do the thing right?

replies.title: Re: Polytheistic ceremonial magic with Wicca
replies.content:
LOL! I literally laughed out loud.

That said, I don’t think it’s an ego thing, at least not in the sense that you describe. If I didn’t believe that you really did know much more about the subject than me, I wouldn’t be asking you in the first place.

That’s why I worded my question in the way I did, i. e. “what is the worst that could happen”.

In this way, I was asking whether my idea was more like pissing in coffee, which, while disguisting, is usually mostly harmless, or whether it was more like someone who knows nothing about chemistry taking a random vial in a chemistry lab and pouring its contents into their coffee, which is quite a bit more dangerous.

replies.title: Re: Polytheistic ceremonial magic with Wicca
replies.content:
Fair enough, but the only answer you're going to get from me is "until you know enough to answer the question yourself, it's not something you have any business worrying about."

My basic rule of thumb is that a student of magic should start by mastering one system of magic exactly as presented by the textbook or the teacher, without messing with it at all. Then get a basic degree of fluency in a couple of other systems. Then, and only then, does it make any kind of sense to start tinkering.

comments.title: Cutting Connections
comments.content:
Good evening, JMG!

I had a couple questions for you tonight...

The first (and more important) actually comes from my wife. Some years back she was initiated into a “clan” (read: small coven) of people practicing a sort of faux-Italian witchcraft (essentially marinara-flavored Wicca). These folks have since turned out to be highly manipulative and abusive, and my wife is no longer associated with them at all. However, there are some lingering connections of considerable potency that she very much wants to be rid of: namely, a leather belt she was given at the initiation (studded with stones and tokens of various signification that she chose herself), a bag in which she kept magical tools, and (probably the most troublesome to deal with) a brand on her right wrist that is shared by the rest of the group and is meant to magically link them to each other and promote a sort of ‘awareness’ of each other’s state of being.

Her current desire and plan is to load the bag, belt inside, with certain cleansing and banishing substances (sage, salt, red peppers), and burn it in our Grove’s Imbolc fire this Saturday, Imbolc being heavily associated with cleansing. She’s written out her intention of being permanently and completely severed from those people on the belt, and removed from it all the stones (since she chose them herself), which are currently buried in black salt. The actual question is: how can she improve this working so as to maximize its efficacy and to minimize any resultant negative raspberry jam? (Our own situation having been rather precarious over the past couple years for a number of reasons, she’s been very leery of any sort of magical blowback.) Also, what if anything can be done about the magical implications of the scar from that brand? Having taken it for a group of people that claimed to be family only to manipulate, control, abuse, and betray her bothers her a lot.

~*~

My second question is of significantly less importance, and is only being paired with the above because I’ve been meaning to ask it for about a month, and keep forgetting to do so before the midnight cutoff! What’s the current status of the Gullna Dagrenning? I’ve been eagerly anticipating it since you first announced working it, but I know you typically field test all your magical coursework, so it could very well be a while. Also, to what extent (if any) have you incorporated the musings of the 17th century Swedish Rosicrucian alchemist and Cabalist Johannes Bureus? I’ve not been able to uncover a very great deal about his adalrunic philosophy yet, but I’m intrigued by the idea of runes and the Tree of Life being conceived of in the same context having roots the go so far back.

Thanks so much for all the effort you put into this every week—it’s a lot of help!
-Scott H. /|\

replies.title: Re: Cutting Connections
replies.content:
1) The working sounds like a very sensible one to me. One thing I'd suggest -- and I assume your wife will do this anyway -- is that the deities invoked in the Imbolc ceremony should be asked for their help in breaking the connection. Make them a special offering if that's something you do in your tradition. Gods are better at this sort of thing than humans!

With regard to the brand, can the two of you afford minor plastic surgery? The most complete way to break the link would be to remove the brand, and that's easy to do with current surgical technique. Ask around and you can probably get it done fairly inexpensively.

2) It's still very much in the works. I recently got from my coauthor a revised version of the ritual that will replace the Rose Cross Ritual -- it's a wowser -- and sent him a complete set of elemental pentagram workings and invocations of the elemental spirits. We still have a chunk of work to do, but it's proceeding steadily.

With regard to Bureus, that's an interesting point and one that I actually hadn't considered. Do you know -- or do any of my readers know -- if it's possible to get a scan of Adalruna Rediviva online? I read Renaissance Latin very nearly as easily as English these days, and I have a pretty fair understanding of Renaissance and early modern occultism, including the Rosicrucian tradition; I've considered more than once doing an annotated translation of Bureus' work. If anyone can point me to a source, that would be helpful.

replies.title: Re: Cutting Connections
replies.content:
1) The one thing I’d forgotten to mention that she’d also done was to include flowers as prayers from her weekly offerings to Bast (her primary tradition, and the one she’s practiced for far longer before taking up with the above-described Strega, is Kemetic Orthodoxy through the House of Netjer, but as a matter of geography and fellowship is obliged to celebrate some rituals with us Druids ;-) ). I’m sure Brigid will also be petitioned during the rite.

2) I sadly have no idea as yet where to find a primary text of Adulruna Rediviva. I do know that both Stephen Flowers and Thomas Karlsson have written on him, but I’ve not read their works yet (Flowers’s appears to be very hard to find, and Karlsson’s is being published next month—links below). Both are are also a bit more Left Hand / Qlippoth-happy for I think either of our tastes. I could point out a very beautiful symphonic album (‘Gothic Kabbalah’ by Therion) with lyrics by Karlsson that caught my eye ages ago before I knew even the slightest thing about runes or occultism, and my appreciation for it has only deepened since.

Nightside of the Runes: Uthark, Adulruna, and the Gothic Cabbala https://www.amazon.com/dp/1620557746/ref=cm_sw_r_cp_api_i_2gxrCb9KNKX9Z

Johannes Bureus and Adalruna: Being a Study Toward the Delineation of the Historical Movement Toward the Northern Dawn Vol. 1 https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000WQB89E/ref=cm_sw_r_cp_api_i_MlxrCb4BE8SNP

Thanks again for the advice!
-Scott H. /|\

replies.title: Re: Cutting Connections
replies.content: Bast and Brigid are very helpful deities for this kind of situation, so she should have no trouble.

replies.title: Re: Cutting Connections
replies.content:
And, boom! Apparently there's nothing like looking if one hopes to actually find something! Here's a link to a beautiful PDF scan of Bureus's complete handwritten text of Adulruna Rediviva:

https://ia801202.us.archive.org/18/items/NLS13A012380/NLS13A012380.pdf

I'll be fascinated to see what you make of it; my own Latin is nothing like up to that level of fluency, so the most I can currently do with this is gaze at it in wonder and longing.

As for the scar--I showed your suggestion to my wife and she was struck by how simple and 'free-of-frills' it was! It might be a minute before we can afford it, but it's certainly a great suggestion that will be born in mind down the road!

-Scott H. /|\

replies.title: Re: Cutting Connections
replies.content: Got it and thank you! It's extremely legible, and I found myself able to read it easily. As it's only about eighty pages of actual text, I'm going to consider an annotated translation. No doubt that'll goad somebody in the academic scene to produce a competing translation -- the translation of the Picatrix Chris Warnock and I did certainly had that effect!

replies.title: Re: Cutting Connections
replies.content:
Anyone can downloaded a scan from the Royal Library of Sweden. The actual title is "Adul-Runa Redi-Viva," not "Adalruna ..." The Royal Library's main website is a little tricky to navigate toward the library catalogue, which is called LIBRIS.

So far as I can tell from the Library's records and other available sources, this work was never printed, so the scan was made from Bureus' own handwritten manuscript, written in 1642. It's in a very clear hand, which isn't harder to read than any printed book of the same century.

replies.title: Re: Cutting Connections
replies.content: It really is an exquisite manuscript, isn't it? Bureus' Latin seems at first glance to be tolerably clean, and I've got several runic fonts and the necessary gear to make scans of the various illustrations, so it's a tempting project.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The Celtic Golden Dawn system doesn't work with the planetary spirits and intelligences at all. My suggestion, if you find that the geomantic work and the planetary spirits and intelligences really call to you, is to take a vacation from the CGD material and immerse yourself in the practices in The Art and Practice of Geomancy instead. The instructions in that latter book include methods of meditation, scrying, and ritual magic, and should keep you busy for several years of hard work; then you can return to the CGD with the benefits of that training, and be ready to go on.

If you're going to work with planetary spirits, remember that you need to start by working with their corresponding intelligences first; spirits are blind forces and tend to distribute their blessings without any sense of what might be turned on its head, while intelligences are better able to pay attention to context. Better still, invoke the deity corresponding to the planet and place the whole working under his or her guidance -- the Orphic hymns to the planetary deities, which are included in the back of The Art and Practice of Geomancy, are good for this purpose.

replies.title: Re: Death for those who think death is the end
replies.content: It can be really rough. There you are, outside your physical body but with no way of understanding your condition, unable to communicate with incarnate people, unwilling to communicate with discarnate beings (or even acknowledge their existence) -- and that can go on for quite a while. comments.title: Lacto-ovo vegetarians and magic
comments.content:
This question continues the thread from last week about vegetarians and magical practice.

I am a lifetime lacto-ovo vegetarian. I was raised that way, and I've tried meat several times and always found it an unenjoyable experience, even meat that everyone agreed was particularly good quality.

When it comes to magical practice, how far do you suppose eggs and dairy can take me?

replies.title: Re: Lacto-ovo vegetarians and magic
replies.content: That varies dramatically from person to person. If you were raised vegetarian and your body thrives on that diet, then there's no reason for you to change it. You can certainly practice meditation, divination, and natural magic all you want. As for ritual magic, the thing to watch for -- and you'll want to ask the people close to you to help you watch for it -- is hypersensitivity. If you get into a state where being around somebody angry leaves you unable to function for hours, where you have to exclude more and more things and people from your life to keep an even keel, you have to choose between eating some meat and stopping your practice of ritual magic. The alternative is usually a messy nervous breakdown, followed by the necessary cessation of all magical practice for a good long time, if not permanently.

replies.title: Re: Lacto-ovo vegetarians and magic
replies.content:
Hi JMG,

I feel like I have some of the symptoms of hypersensitivity you describe. I practice ritual magic and am not vegetarian... But I find that my emotions are absolutely haywire if I don't stay well fed, and being in a number of kinds of settings has become more and more challenging-- particularly anywhere that's chaotic or has a lot of people that go through it, from sports events to Air BnBs. Do you know of anything that might help?

replies.title: Re: Lacto-ovo vegetarians and magic
replies.content:
I can only speak for myself, but I am a strict, ethical vegan since July of 2010 and vegetarian since 1991. I don't cheat and eat flesh or dairy because even if I wanted to, I would become very sick having not eaten it in so long.

I have been doing the SoP once a day since last year around this time and I have noticed zero ill effects. Sensitivity has often been a problem in my life, but if anything, the SoP has helped me develop defenses and strategies for coping with it. The SoP has been great for me. I find I am much more resilient, even-tempered, and easygoing than I have ever been in my life before this. Back when I first saw the issue of vegans being advised not to do ritual magic on this board, I prayed to the Druid gods about it. They said "stay watchful, but otherwise leave it in our hands".

Thanks ever so much for Magic Monday.

comments.content:
John--

How does one balance between innovation and exploration on the one hand and respect for traditional methods on the other? Or, put another way, how does one develop new ideas while safeguarding against error? Per usual, both extremes are undesirable: reckless tinkering and lethargic ossification. (Is the former one of those areas where Crowley, for example, went astray?)

Two examples come to mind for me here. First, regarding a question I asked previously about the Kabylion and Da'ath, wherein if ALL is Mind, then how could Knowledge not be the highest attainment. And you had answered, more or less, that the framing of Mind by the New Thought movement was one of that movement's weaknesses. If we cannot assess what aspects of past teaching are solid and which are weaknesses, how can we be sure that anything we build further is on a solid foundation?

A second example is the reworking of the Tree I had started on when first introduced to the Druidic teachings in the Dolmen Arch. The parallel of the three elements (calas, gwyar, nwyfre) and the three columns (form, force, consciousness) struck me as something important and I found myself exploring the notion of the Tree re-envisioned with the three trinaries parallel to one another and stacked, rather like a triangular prism turned on end, with Malkuth centered below and Da'ath centered above. The symmetry had great appeal (and also "promoted" Da'ath, as it were, to the status of closest-to-the-Unmanifest), but a certain wiser head suggested that I first study the traditional lore of kabbalah before messing with it. (Advice, I would add, that I decided to follow.)

Apologies for the long-windedness. I suppose it comes back to wanting to explore and understand, but not wanting to blunder down dead-ends and waste time in the process.

Thanks.

David BTL

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
As one example, I think of all the time and effort (and lives) spent on the development, explication, and expanding of the Ptolemaic model of the cosmos, all of which was ultimately a waste. I'd like to avoid doing something like that.

Of course, as I write this, I wonder if I know that you are going to say something about the fact that I'm asking the wrong questions here. That I'm asking "is it true?" when I ought to be asking "does it work?"

"Tools, not truths," Whomever She May Be once told me in a meditation.

replies.title: Wasted Effort?
replies.content:
Your example points up a couple of issues. First, there was actually a lot of mathematical innovation that was driven by work on the Ptolomaic and similar models. This wasn't thrown out - a lot of it forms part of the basis of our mathematics today.

Second, there's an issue of measurement accuracy. It wasn't until Tycho Brahe's (the Prince of Astronomers) naked-eye observations of the orbit of Mars that there was sufficient accuracy for Kepler to find that an ellipse with the Sun at one focus was a better approximation than the then-current composite geo- helio-centric model. (In case you're wondering, Copernicus was just another Ptolomy-style system that changed the origin.)

All previous observations were not accurate enough, and subsequent observations were too accurate: a simple ellipse wouldn't work.

John Roth

replies.title: Re: Wasted Effort?
replies.content:
John R--

Understood. And you make excellent points. This actually touches on the second part of that follow-on post I made about asking the wrong question.

My instinctive reaction is still along the lines of "but they were still wrong" -- that is chasing a mirage that wasn't true, even if tools were developed that proved useful in other venues. If one is seeking Truth, Reality, and all that jazz, then error, particularly fundamental error of concept, is unacceptable. (I might, for example, develop useful tools in the exposition of my flat-earth model, but I'm still a flat-earther at the end of the day.)

But as I came to by the end of that follow-on post, if one is "merely" (a loaded term, if there was one) seeking tools that function (that is, a useful map of the territory that will help one navigate, rather than an absolute understanding of that territory) then those kinds of processes, blind-alleys, wandering approximations, and mistaken models are more acceptable.

It is a matter of framing, I guess.

David BTL

replies.title: Re: Wasted Effort?
replies.content:
Can we ever know The Truth in any real sense? A lot of philosophical and spiritual systems say no. The cosmology that I work with, for example, has parallel realities that split and merge continuously as one of its fundamental ideas. How could this work with our current understanding of physical reality? I have no idea, frankly, other than that there's a whole lot of illusion somewhere.

The pursuit of The Truth can be beneficial for a lot of people in a lot of lifetimes, and I suppose there are benefits to be gained from a spiritual evolution viewpoint over numerous lifetimes, but for the finite to understand the Infinite in any real sense is simply not possible.

Or so I understand.

replies.title: Re: Wasted Effort?
replies.content:
The pursuit of Truth (and Truth as Knowledge) is a trap into which I repeatedly fall. Accepting our utter cluelessness is part of my path here, I'm coming to realize.

David BTL

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
That's one of those huge questions that has no simple answer. My basic rule, which has worked well for me and for the students of mine who've followed it, is to start out by taking up one traditional system and mastering it thoroughly, top to bottom, so that you understand it at a gut level. Then learn at least one other system, preferably as different from the first one as possible. (For me, the two systems were respectively Golden Dawn ceremonial magic and traditional Southern rootwork.) By the time you've done that, you'll have a good personal grasp of what's important and what's not, and can begin tinkering with things intelligently.

The metaphor of a foundation and a building isn't actually that helpful for understanding the work of magical training. Think of it rather as doing an archeological dig in search of a lost city. All you're doing in the first phases of the work is clearing away the turf and the topsoil, laying out the grid, and beginning to record and catalog everything you find -- all things that can and should be done by rote. Later on, you start coming across the first traces of the lost city you're excavating, and you start adjusting what you're doing in accordance with that. By the time you've worked your way down to what used to be street level, you've marked off certain areas as "don't bother" and let yourself be guided entirely by what you've found.

replies.content: No, it's utterly impossible to communicate without making assumptions. As the philosopher Michael Polanyi showed quite some time ago, every piece of information we can state explicitly gets most of its meaning from a network of assumptions and presuppositions that are always unstated, and this "tacit knowledge" (his term) can't be stated without dragging in even more assumptions and presuppositions. comments.title: Geomancy for a medical issue
comments.content:
From Goats and Roses

I have a question about a geomancy reading I did for a medical procedure being considered. I did the reading for another person, who was there as I did it. In that case, is it correct to just read it straight up, and not shift the houses as I would if I were doing the reading about the person, without their knowledge? I did the reading without shifting.
I have another question but I will give the data first:

If we go ahead with the (medical treatment under consideration) as planned with (hospital and surgeon) will my (medical condition) decrease significantly?
First Laeticia
2nd Fortuna Major
3rd Carcer
4th Albus
5th Amissio
6th Populus
7th Aquisitio
8th Conjunctio
9th Puer
10th Puella
11th Amissio
12th Fortuna Major
Right Witness Populus
Left Witness Carcer
Judge Carcer

At first I thought the reading looked positive, because Carcer could indicate that this condition can be contained.

From the geomancy book, the 6th is the condition, the 7th the doctor, and the 10th the treatment. So Populus describes the condition, as this person has frequent episodes which make ordinary life difficult. For the doctor, Aquisitio, I read this as good, we will gain from this person, but also the surgeon will gain (this is a well-known treatment, but this will be the first time the treatment has been used for this specific condition and the doc is eager to try it out), and the 10th Puella. My biggest question is, does this indicate that the treatment will work, but only briefly? The 6th and 10th perfect by translation, the amissio in 11th and 5th. Does the fact they perfect indicate a generally good outcome, or should I consider the amissio in the 11th and 5? And if so, what do they indicate?

Also is there anything else that sheds light on the reading that I missed. Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Geomancy for a medical issue
replies.content:
R.W. Populus(querent) will follow L.W. Carcer(quesited) when it comes to stability. The Judge confirms stability as the outcome. The patient might end up in the same place he/she is now.

9th is also Puella, right?
So the 10th perfects with the 6th by translation (Amissio) and it also perfects by Company simple with the 9th.

But perhaps the thing to note is the lack of perfection between 1st and 6th houses.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy for a medical issue
replies.content:
The first house is the querent, which means the person for whom the chart is cast, whoever that is. Thus you're right that you don't rotate it.

Second, you asked a yes or no question. In a house chart, the answer to a yes or no question is indicated by the presence or absence of perfection between the house of the querent (1st) and the house of the quesited (in this case, the 6th, since it's a health-related question). No mode of perfection links these two figures -- in fact, neither of them appears anywhere else in the chart -- and so the answer is no, the procedure will not cause the condition to decrease significantly. All other factors are secondary to that.

Third, all Acquisitio in the seventh tells you is that the doctor is going to profit from the procedure.

comments.content:
Last week you said, "In the broadest sense, the creation of a fully formed mental body is the task for which we're here as human beings. The process of gaining the ability to reflect on our thoughts and our feelings, so that we can see how we're making ourselves miserable, is a very important part of that process of creation. Magic is one set of tools for doing that. There are many, many others."

Can you comment on what some of those other ways are? Are some more effective than others?

- Dylan

replies.title: Re: different ways to the same goal?
replies.content: Every form of spiritual practice, every kind of education that works with the mind, and the ordinary everyday experiences of life in the world all help form the mental body. Which ones are more effective than others depends entirely on personal factors.

comments.title: Teenagers
comments.content: What if any are the occult layers of becoming a teen? I have a pre-teen daughter and a son that’s not too far behind. She has shown no interest in paganism liking more Christianity and my son is the opposite.

replies.title: Re: Teenagers
replies.content: Puberty is the point at which the desire body -- the lower aspect of the astral body -- awakens, and the channels of subtle energy in the etheric body go haywire. The same life force that expresses itself in sex is also what is used in magic. Thus teens very often find themselves drawn to this or that spiritual path as the first stirrings of puberty get under way. As long as they're not getting into something actively dangerous, let them; your daughter may have karmic reasons for getting into Christianity, and of course it makes a great way to work out adolescent rebelliousness in a Pagan household. If you can find something innocuous to forbid them, that will help. (My dad absolutely forbade me to grow my hair long, and so that became the focus of my teenage resentments; the results were utterly harmless.)

comments.title: Thank you
comments.content:
JMG, Thank you for everything.. hahaha! comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
This is a bit embarrassing, but here goes: for the past while I've been practicing the polytheist LBRP and Middle Pillar and working with Hellenic deities. I have loved this and found it really rewarding. I then meditated on the tarot and found it helpful for understanding the Tree of Life. Finding the Cabala fascinating and elucidating I began doing the work as outlined in _Paths of Wisdom_. For 40 days I meditated on the Sephiroth, Kether to Malkuth and back again. Then I began on the Magical Image of Malkuth, did the five meditations on the Unbalanced Force of Malkuth and have today completed the 3rd meditation on the Divine Name.

What I found today was interesting; the Divine Name seemed to be unhappy with me and seemed to communicate to my mind that He didn't like that I was working with a different pantheon while meditating on his name. I left the meditation feeling reprimanded, and feeling that other times when I'd used the name in conjunction with the Magical Image I'd been ignoring pointed and obvious reprimands. I felt pretty clearly that I can either work with Him or I can work with Hellenic deities but I can't have it both ways. I'm not sure if other people have had this experience, so I guess now that I'm a data point!

The issue I have with this is when I've tried the traditional Golden Dawn material I felt nauseous, and am not on the level of translating the entire symbolic system of the Cabala into Hellenic symbolism. This is, I imagine, a large part of why you warn not to mix systems. I thought I would be more easily able to get away with praying and working with one pantheon and then meditating on something else, but I think I may have reached a point where I can't continue with this work, and may need to find a new set of themes to meditate on, top on my list is the detailed meditations instructions you give in _The Art and Practice of Geomancy_. Before beginning though do you think that there would be any reason to think that meditating on Geomancy would likely cause any problems when combined with Hellenic devotional worship, LBRP and the Middle Pillar? My intuition says 'everything should be fine,' but I feel a bit humbled all things considered, and so wish to run it by you.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
If I may--this feeling is a long-noted characteristic of the Jewish/Christian deity. He is a jealous god, and is not willing to have his devotees worship others. In my mind, it's a "pick one": you can worship the god of Abraham, or you can be polytheist--but the two go together like hot sauce and pudding.

The Abrahamic traditions get around this by prayers to angels and saints, who are clearly understood as not-gods: this seems to be acceptable.

SamChevre

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Many thanks, SamChevre -- that's pretty much what He told me.  I know some Christians with very strong polytheistic leaning and foolishly thought it could work.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, first, SamChevre is quite correct. The god of Israel is a jealous god; if you work with him, you can't work with other gods. Meditating on his names while invoking Greek deities is not a way to get good results.

Second, the meditation instructions in The Art and Practice of Geomancy are entirely suitable for a Hellene, and geomancy is pretty nondenominational and should be fine. You might also consider meditating on the Greek myths -- that's absolutely classic work for Hellenic practitioners, and very much worth doing.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Points very well taken -- thank you!

replies.title: a jealous god
replies.content:
Without disputing what anyone has said, I would like to add some personal data points. My familial religion is Judaism. I was brought up in a liberal denomination which at that time, for understandable reasons, was rather spiritually empty (PTSD on a massive scale). It has gotten a lot better since, but I didn't know that was going to happen. I tried without success to form a relationship with the God of Israel.

As a young adult, I took up Goddess worship and became a Witch of the Wiccan sort. For the first time I was able to pray and have a sense that Someone was listening. The Craft (apologies to you Freemasons) offered practices and ways of thinking that helped me greatly in my spiritual quest. However, almost from the start I was aware of how young and undeveloped a religion Wicca is compared to Judaism.

My new-found religious education actually made it easier for me to get something out of religious services when I attended them with my family. Late in life, I took up the practice of lighting the Sabbath candles at home, saying the kindergarten version of the traditional blessings over the candles, wine and bread, and praying briefly to the god of my ancestors when I did this, sometimes expressing gratitude, sometimes asking for help for my loved ones or for the United States. So far, Adonai seems to be tolerating my prayers. It isn't in me to give love by command, but it may be that regular practice of one of the more important mitzvot will open my heart eventually.

Of course, by doing this I'm being a good Pagan. Failing to give reverence to the Being worshipped by my ancestors would be disrespectful and bad luck besides.

I have always kept my magical practices separate and pretty strictly pagan. I don't do the Golden Dawn version of the LBRP or any other banishings that use Hebrew words or acronyms. I mostly avoid Kabbalah. I have one set of candlesticks for the Shabbat candles and other sets for my polytheist altar, which is in a different room. (Separating this from that is a very Jewish practice.) Of course many of my Wiccan observances are strictly forbidden by post-Exilic Judaism, not much I can do about that, but there are many areas of commonality too.

comments.title: Meditations on the Old ones
comments.content:
Good day! I was thinking about the eldritch modifications to the SoP, which got me thinking about the nature of deities and forming relationships with them.

For one thing, using names derived from modern fantasy, but still having a specific intention and symbolism attached to the names, strikes me as potentially a really respectful way of working with deities. Especially if one isn't called to a particular historical pantheon.

For example, if I have done some meditation on Water, and how it relates to spiritual growth and other conscious experiences, then when I invoke Cthulhu in that station, then there may be a deity willing to serve that role, without me presuming there to be one. Rather than assume (for example) that Esus is willing to work with me, I utter the invocation to the Great Dreamer. My consciousness and will is focused on the symbolism of Water, so perhaps some appropriate deity that isn't picky about names is willing to answer the call. (Unless of course, Cthulhu really does hear me in his dreams :D). Basically, you leave it to the deity to respond without invoking their historical name.

I know I'm making some heavy handed assumptions about what it means to form a relationship with deities, but it was a novel and exciting thought for me, and was curious on your take on it. I'm wondering just where this train of thought will leave me...

Many thanks as always!

Cthulhu fhtang!

replies.title: Re: Meditations on the Old ones
replies.content:
I have very mixed feelings about working with imagery out of fantasy fiction. On the one hand, I've seen a fantastic amount of half-witted magic done with notions out of fantasy -- I'm recalling here in particular "Klingon Wicca," which was fashionable on the West Coast for a little while, and the people who tried to use Katherine Kurtz' Deryni magic. They pretty reliably didn't get results -- I suspect it was because none of them had any Deryni blood. ;-)

On the other hand, Lovecraft and the other writers in his circle seem to have tapped into something surprisingly potent. The way that Cthulhu has become a massive presence in pop culture is indicative: there's a real archetype at work here, and where there's an archetype, there's something deeper. (I tend to think of archetypes these days as the reflections of the gods in the human unconscious.)

That said, there's a precedent for what you're suggesting. Pietro d'Abano says that you should invoke spirits by their titles and roles instead of their names, since the names change over time, and until you've made contact with your guardian angel and received from him the material to put into your personal Book of Spirits, you don't know the right names to use. So it may be worth trying out.

Note 1: Cthulhu's far from the only fictional creation from that period that has potent archetypal connections. I once succeeded, entirely by accident, in evoking Batman; the results were pretty remarkable.

Note 2: It's also by no means unheard of for a fictional deity to become, all of a sudden, noticeably less fiction. The merry pranksters who launched the Reformed Druids of North America (RDNA) invented a wholly made-up god, Dalon ap Landu, God of the Groves, for their ritual. I'm far from the only person who's encountered him in the course of doing RDNA ritual. Did some other woodland god, perhaps Silvanus, decide to answer the invocation? Or is something else going on? Nobody knows.

replies.title: Re: Meditations on the Old ones
replies.content: If it is possible could you tell more about the Batman story that sounded really interesting.

replies.title: Re: Meditations on the Old ones
replies.content: JMG, please tell us more of the Batman incident—sounds fascinating!

replies.title: Re: Klingon Wicca
replies.content:
I once had the pleasure of attending a Klingon Wicca ritual that had been created by some NROOGD initiates. They took the basic ritual of the NROOGD tradition of witchcraft, its structure, style and magical intent, and adapted it to Klingon symbology, manners, cultural attitudes, etc. The people who did this were very familiar with both NROOGD witchcraft and the Star Trek mythos, and approached the adaptation the way you have been developing the Celtic Golden Dawn.

The resulting ritual was performed deadpan. It was a workable magical ritual, authentically Klingon, and for anyone who was sufficiently familiar with the underlying NROOGD ritual, hilarious.

comments.title: Horescope
comments.content:
Hi JMG

I have Mars in Taurus and Venus in Aries. Does the mutual reception mitigate the fact that they are both in detriment?

Also is there anyway of improving detrimented planets? I have Saturn in Aries too. This is exactly conjunct my north node. I feel stymied in going for what I truly want in life. Is this a karmic debt of some kind? What to do??

Thanks

replies.title: Re: Horescope
replies.content:
1) In traditional astrology, it doesn't just mitigate the detriment, it erases it. Given that mutual reception, Mars acts as though it's in Aries, and Venus as though it's in Taurus. Pay attention to yourself, and you'll notice the way that the two planetary forces strengthen and help out each other.

2) That, on the other hand, you're going to have to deal with all your life. You can still achieve the things you want, but it's going to take you more effort than most people have to put in, and there are things you will never be able to achieve. That's an experience that every soul has to have at some point; the fact that you're getting it out of the way in this life means that you won't have to wrestle with it in a future life.

comments.title: addiction
comments.content:
JMG-

Part of my magical path (GD) has been realizing I was an alcoholic. I'm very lucky in that I figured this out long before I did any serious damage to my life, before I did any really. I'm very thankful for that, and quitting drinking has been pretty easy so far actually, much helped by some discursive meditation and journaling.

My question is, there is definitely a biochemical component to addiction, my brain wants dopamine. That's easy enough to deal with (for me anyway), but what are the magical dimension of addiction? Are there negative energies attached to me that I should be working on getting rid of? I do a daily LBRP, and recently started working with the Middle Pillar exercise. Is there anything you would recommend adding? (I should probably note I have children 4,6 in the house).

Thank you very much for providing this resource. And by the way, are you compiling all this somewhere? Seems like a searchable database might be very helpful for people.

replies.title: Re: addiction
replies.content:
Regular practice of meditation and basic rituals such as the LBRP and Middle Pillar will help, though there's always going to be that dopamine response. On a magical level, an addiction is a thoughtform, a pattern on the astral plane that will tend to draw your thoughts and feelings along the track it's laid down. That can be erased over time by magical and meditative work, and also by building up an alternative thoughtform that guides you in different directions -- that's why establishing new habits that don't involve alcohol is so useful to the recovering alcoholic.

No, I haven't tried compiling all this -- I simply don't have the time. If someone else were to do it, that would be great.

comments.title: Geomancy
comments.content:
JMG,

Rescently I've been studying and casting a few house charts to expand my Geomancy skills a bit. I understand where the winnesses and the judge fit in when doing a daily, weekly, mothly, etc. chart. However I'm a bit confused on how they fit into a chart with a specific question. I noticed that you interpreted a few example chatrts in *The Art and Practice of Geomancy* without the witnesses and judge, and this seems more clear to me. At the end of the section though you say that the witnesses and judge are interpreted as in the shield chart. replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content: When you do the house chart, pay attention to the house chart, and use the first house and the house of the quesited to make your judgment. When you do the shield chart, pay attention to the shield chart, and use the judge and witnesses to make your judgment. Mixing the two generally produces confusion. If I were to rewrite my geomancy books, I'd include something to that effect in twenty-point type, boldface.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:

"The Judge and Witnesses from the basic chart, of course, also provide an overall view of the situation. Just as in a shield chart reading, the Judge represents the final outcome of the situation, while the Witnesses stand for the querent (the Right Witness) and quesited (the Left Witness) in general."

a lot of the house charts have two witnesses and the judge in the middle, some do not. Your response from above seems like you're saying not to use the witnesses and judge in a house chart. comments.title: Magic and children
comments.content: Perusing the comments from a while back I saw something about magic and children, where you explained that, while there are certain precautions to take, many people get overly panicky about it. I'm wondering from primarily a curiosity standpoint (though I do have a young child at home, I'm not doing any ceremonial magic these days): precisely why are those precautions necessary? What is it about children that makes them more sensitive/receptive to magical influences? I could probably speculate a few plausible reasons, but I'm interested in your understandings. Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Magic and children
replies.content: The different bodies we have awaken and finish forming at different times in the course of growing up. It's a gradual process, layer by layer, and before any given body's finished forming, the energies brought through in serious ritual magic can interfere with their normal development. With quite young children there's also the risk that an unhealthy connection can be established between the child and a spirit -- this rarely leads to full-blown possession, but it can have a range of unfortunate results even lacking that.

comments.title: Some questions
comments.content:
Archdruid,

My martial arts club practices traditional Chinese kung fu (Chow Gar Southern Mantis), which clearly has an esoteric system that used to be a part of it. However my sifu, and his sifu, are from materialist schools and consider esoteric systems "woo woo." I'm not sure what our lineage's master believes because he lives in England and doesn't really travel to the US, but my teacher doesn't seem particularly interested in studying the esoteric principals of the system. He's very good at teaching the physical (iron body, forms, shock power, practical application) and mental (visualization and stoicism), but I feel that without the esoteric principals there is a lack of wholeness. Since I'm only learning the physical and metal sides of this system, would it be safe for me to find ways to introduce or mix the druid esoteric principals with this martial arts?

Thank you for taking the time to guide us in our practices!

Regards,

Syfen

replies.title: Re: Some questions
replies.content:
It might be better to try to find out what you can about the esoteric dimension of your art itself. Chow Gar, according to the sources I found via a search engine, traces its lineage back to the southern Shaolin tradition, and so it's probably a safe bet that it's got the standard core of Shaolin spirituality, which is Ch'an (i.e., Zen) Buddhism. (Da Mo aka Bodhidharma, who introduced martial arts to the original Shaolin monastery, was also the teacher who brought Zen from India to China.)

One angle you could pursue is to see if you can get an image -- photo or painting are equally fine -- of the founder of your style. Set up a little shrine and burn incense before the image, bow as you would to your sifu, and ask the founder to guide you to the appropriate esoteric teachings. That's a very traditional approach and seems to have good results!

comments.title: Fixing things
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I was a long-time reader of the Archdruid Report. Even invited you to a Transition Town "unleashing" I was organizing up in MA. In the course of my environmental journey, I took up repairing various and sundry items. Our economy is so great, just now, at producing things that are actually just a simulacrum of the thing that I have had no trouble finding things in need of just a (often) minor repair to bring back into use. In fact, other than patching bike tubes, I've almost never fixed the same appliance or electronic twice b/c there's such an abundance of different, but equally-shoddily-made ones around.

So on the one hand this is a great Green Wizardry and resilience building skill and it's environmentally friendly and saves money.

On the other hand there may be some thing else (partially) at work. I'd always been a tinkerer growing up, but a major breakup definitely gave me a small kick in the "fixing things" direction. Time passed, I largely got over it and I still like repairing things so I figure that's O.K.

But the last two months have been... *strange*. I spent 12 mo away from home in San Francisco and when I returned 2 months ago, several not-so-minor repairs came up, about once a week. The house was in full use while I was gone, mind you. 1st, the upright freezer stopped running (only 3 yrs old). It ended up being just the mechanical thermostat... but I was the one to discover it has stopped running. A week later, all of the outlets in one of the bedrooms stopped working. We still haven't figured out the cause. A week after that, the 50" TV stopped turning on (not a loss, in my book, but near and dear to one roommate :).

Things have settled, but I couldn't help but wonder how to take this! Is my notion of one day making a living repairing things doomed to failure? Is the problem that I haven't committed to this notion and thus, it's more a fantasy than an actual goal?
Or is this a good sign, that the universe will make sure there is an abundance of clients at my door? (the freezer was a simple fix, but the room is hard to trace and TV could be a lot of work)
Or were these devices "holding their breath" for my return? :)

I suppose, since all the issues are electrical, maybe these are all just symptoms of some bad wiring and nothing magical...

Any insight would be helpful! At least, I hope the story is entertaining to you and the readers. Insight and recommended cleansing protocol/rituals would be appreciated. Any deities I should get right with? 1961 house in what was an orchard a century or more ago, currently inhabited by Jews from the former soviet union (if that changes anything!).

Thanks,
Alex

replies.title: Re: Fixing things
replies.content: My guess is that you're being tested. The fact that you dealt with each of the problems as it came up is a good sign. My guess, in fact, is that you'll do very well as a repairman, and you should go ahead and get the knowledge you need to do it well -- for example, the problem with the bedroom outlets may be trying to tell you that you need to know more about electrical systems!

comments.title: Buddhist practice + geomancy
comments.content: I've been doing Tibetan Buddhist practices involving reciting prayers. It's more mystical in orientation than magical but there are some subtle body aspects to it. Do you think I can safely do the practices in The Art And Practice of Geomancy together with my existing practices? I've done some divinations with good results, but I haven't done any of the spirit work yet.

replies.title: Re: Buddhist practice + geomancy
replies.content: Do you have a teacher who's instructing you in the Tibetan Buddhist practices? If so, talk to the teacher. I have no background in Tibetan Buddhism at all, and thus don't have the knowledge base that would allow me to answer your question; an experienced Tibetan Buddhist practitioner will have that knowledge.
